Day 10: Naples to Brindisi

All-Day Train Journey into Puglia

itinerary
travel
trains
puglia
naples
Published

June 14, 2026

Overview

Travel day: Depart Naples by train and arrive in Brindisi, gateway to our Puglia tour. The train ride is not a burden — it’s one of Italy’s great scenic journeys, crossing the Apennines and rolling across the Tavoliere plains before reaching the Adriatic coast.

Train: Trenitalia Intercity (IC) — direct, no changes, First Class available Duration: ~5 hours 30 minutes Distance: ~380 km (Naples → Foggia → Bari → Brindisi)

TipNot a Milk Run

The Intercity (IC) direct train is a proper express service — not the slow regional train that stops at every village. It calls only at major stations (Caserta, Foggia, Bari, a few coastal stops). First Class is available and worth booking for a long day. See Trains Research for full details and booking links.

Morning: Check Out & Station (7–9 AM)

Hotel Checkout

  • Confirm checkout time with hotel (usually 11 AM, but early options available)
  • If taking a morning train: Arrange bags the night before; request early checkout or luggage storage if needed
  • Final walk — get one last coffee at your Naples bar

Getting to Napoli Centrale

From most Naples accommodations:

Option Time Notes
Walk 10–25 min depending on hotel location Fine for light luggage
Metro Line 1 5–10 min To Garibaldi/Centrale stop
Taxi 10–15 min €10–15; use official white taxis only
WarningNapoli Centrale — Watch Your Belongings

The station and surrounding Piazza Garibaldi are busy and attract pickpockets. Keep bags zipped, phones in pockets, and stay alert. Once you’re on the train, you can relax.

Arrive at station: 20–30 minutes before departure - Check departure board for Binario (platform number) - Intercity trains show “IC” prefix in the schedule - Your ticket has the car (carrozza) and seat number — find your car before boarding

The Train Journey (approx. 5.5 hours)

Recommended Seat Strategy

Sit on the LEFT side of the train (facing direction of travel, left window) after Foggia — this gives you Adriatic Sea views as the train hugs the coastline into Brindisi.

For the Naples → Foggia leg: Either side is fine (inland route, mountains and plains).

What You’ll Pass Through

The journey is a geographic cross-section of southern Italy:

Segment What You See Approx. Time from Naples
Naples → Caserta Sprawling metro area 30 min
Caserta Reggia di Caserta visible (royal palace) ~35 min
Campanian Plains Volcanic soil, agriculture, volcanic hills 45–60 min
Benevento area Apennine foothills, Roman archway in town ~1h
Apennine crossing Mountains, tunnels, dramatic drops 1–2h
Foggia Enter Puglia! Flat Tavoliere plains begin ~2h 30m
Tavoliere delle Puglie Vast wheat plains — “the breadbasket of Italy” 2.5–3h
Bari Puglia’s capital, brief stop ~3h 30m
Polignano a Mare Dramatic cliffs over Adriatic visible from train ~4h
Fasano / Ostuni area Hilltop white towns in distance 4–4.5h
Brindisi Historic port city, Adriatic arrival ~5h 30m

Foggia: You’ve Entered Puglia

Foggia is the moment the landscape transforms — the Apennines end, the world opens up, and you’re on the Tavoliere, a vast ancient sea floor turned wheat plain. This is the beginning of the south’s distinctive feel: big sky, limestone, olive trees, whitewashed architecture on distant hilltops.

Bari Stop (~3.5 hours in)

The train stops briefly at Bari Centrale. You don’t change trains — this is a through stop. But it’s a good moment to: - Stretch - Grab something from the café car if you haven’t - Look out at Bari’s coastline

Note: If you wanted to break up the journey and spend time in Bari, this route allows that (get off here, explore for an afternoon, take a later regional train to Brindisi). See Trains Research — Option B for the Bari stopover option.

What to Bring for the Train

Arrival in Brindisi (~2–3 PM)

Brindisi Station

Brindisi Stazione is a modest, manageable station — nothing like the chaos of Napoli Centrale. Easy arrival.

  • 10-minute walk to the historic city center
  • Taxi stand outside the station (€8–12 to most hotels)
  • Small station — luggage storage is limited; go to hotel first

Brindisi: First Impressions

Brindisi has been a major Mediterranean port since Roman times — it’s where Virgil died, where Crusaders departed for Jerusalem, where the Appian Way ended. It’s not a tourist hotspot, which is part of its charm.

What you’ll notice: - Calm, unhurried pace - Working port atmosphere - Beautiful inner harbor (Seno di Ponente) - Fewer English menus — more authentic - Reasonable prices

Afternoon: Brindisi Arrival & Orientation (3–6 PM)

Check In

Get to your hotel/accommodation, drop bags, freshen up after the train. This is a recovery and orientation afternoon.

Optional: Walk the Inner Harbor

Brindisi’s waterfront is compact and walkable:

  • Colonne Romane — Two Roman columns marking the end of the Appian Way (via Appia); only one survives intact. This is where ancient Rome ended.
  • Seno di Ponente — The sheltered inner harbor; watch fishing boats and ferries
  • Castello Svevo — Norman-era castle overlooking the harbor (exterior worth seeing)
  • Piazza Vittorio Emanuele — Main square near the waterfront
  • Corso Garibaldi — Main pedestrian street into town

Walk time: 60–90 minutes easy at a relaxed pace

NoteBrindisi is a Gateway, Not a Destination

You don’t need to see everything today. Brindisi is where you begin the Puglia tour — save energy for the days ahead. A light afternoon, an early dinner, and an early night is the right call after a long travel day.

If You’re Curious: The Columns

Colonna Romana (just one column remains standing) - Via Colonne, overlooking the harbor - Free to view (exterior) - Marks the official terminus of Via Appia (Appian Way) — the road from Rome - Julius Caesar walked this. Cicero, Horace, Augustus Caesar. - Simple stone column; enormous historical weight

Evening: Dinner in Brindisi (7–9 PM)

Welcome to Puglia — the food changes immediately. This is the heel of the boot, where the cuisine is distinct from Naples:

Puglia staples you’ll start seeing:

Dish Description
Orecchiette “Little ears” pasta, often with turnip greens (cime di rapa) or ragù
Burrata Fresh mozzarella filled with cream — Puglia’s gift to the world
Fave e cicorie Fava bean purée with bitter greens — ancient peasant dish, extraordinary
Ciceri e tria Pasta with fried and boiled chickpeas — old Leccese recipe
Primitivo Local red wine — rich, full-bodied, from Manduria
Negroamaro Another Puglian red — darker, more tannic

Seafood: - Brindisi is a fishing port — the fresh catch is exceptional - Ricci di mare (sea urchin) if in season - Polpo (octopus) grilled or in salad

Where to look: - Walk toward the harbor and away from the station - Corso Garibaldi and side streets into centro storico - Look for family-run trattorie over tourist-facing restaurants

Evening: Prepare for the Tour

Tonight, get organized for the Puglia days ahead:

Key Links

  • Trains Research — Naples to Brindisi details
